nativeRange.habitat: Seasonal wetlands, marshes, and riverbanks across tropical West and Central Africa

decorPreferences: open background placement with room to grow tall
bright lighting to develop red coloration

summary: A demanding red-to-pink background stem plant from tropical West Africa; generally considered a showpiece red Ammannia for high-tech planted tanks where strong light, CO2, and iron-rich fertilization bring out wavy copper-red foliage.
careNotes: Ammannia gracilis is a tall stem plant from tropical West and Central Africa (Senegal, Mali, Guinea, and neighbouring countries) and is sold in the hobby as 'Red Ammannia', 'Large Ammania', or 'Pink Ammania'. It is larger-leaved and more upright than its smaller relative Ammannia senegalensis, and is typically used as a background accent in 60 cm+ planted tanks. Sources broadly agree on a temperature range around 22-28 °C and soft-to-moderately-hard water, with pH most commonly cited as 6.0-7.5 (some sources extend to pH 5-8). Adult height is the main point of cross-source disagreement: Buce Plant cites 5-8 in (~13-20 cm), Aquarium Co-Op describes it as 'over 10 in' (>25 cm), and aquariumbreeder.com and aqua-fish.net both report 20-50 cm under cultivation — averaged here to 20-50 cm because the taller end is the species' natural submersed habit under proper conditions. To develop the characteristic copper-red to pink coloration the plant is sold for, it generally needs high light (~30-50 PAR, 8-10 h photoperiod), pressurized CO2 (~25-30 mg/L), and consistent iron + micronutrient dosing alongside macro fertilization; with low light it survives but stays pale yellow-green and may drop lower leaves. Best red colour is reported when nitrates run low while phosphate and micros remain plentiful. The plant prefers a nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil or root tabs) and is sensitive to parameter swings — sudden changes can trigger leaf melt, especially on freshly planted tissue-culture stems transitioning from emersed to submersed growth. Note: Tropica did not have a dedicated species page accessible during research (the search URL returned no per-species detail), so the primary source role was filled by Buce Plant and supplementary aquarium-hobby care guides were used for cross-verification.
breedingNotes: Propagated by stem cuttings: cut the newest growth (about 5-10 cm with a few leaves) from a mature stem and replant in the substrate. Trimming the upper half of an established stem encourages bushier lateral side-shoot growth on the mother plant. When grown emersed, the plant produces small purple flowers, but seed propagation is uncommon in the hobby.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Used for family (Lythraceae), West African origin, care level (medium-difficult), light (high), CO2 (recommended, 25-30 ppm), growth rate (fast), placement (mid-background), pH (5-7.5, soft acidic preferred), propagation, and iron/micro fertilization. Primary role assigned to Buce Plant because the Tropica search URL supplied in the manifest returned no per-species detail page for Ammannia gracilis — Tropica does not currently appear to list this species (their Ammannia catalogue page shows A. crassicaulis instead).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked placement (background), growth rate (fast), height (>10 in / >25 cm), light/CO2 producing red-to-pink coloration vs yellow-green under medium light, and Easy Green / iron fertilization.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (aquariumbreeder.com)
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (West/Central Africa, Senegal, Mali, Guinea, etc.), adult size (25-50 cm), temperature (22-26 °C), pH (6.5-7.5), GH (2-10), light (30-50 PAR), CO2 (required), minimum tank size (~80 L / 20 gal), and propagation by 5-8 cm cuttings.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Lythraceae), West African origin (Senegal wetlands/marshes), size (20-50 cm), temperature (20-28 °C), pH (6-8), hardness (4-12 dGH), high light, CO2 supplementation, gravel/nutrient substrate, and 10 cm stem-cutting propagation.
